content:
**Executive summary:** Reproduction: biological process by which living organisms produce offspring of their own kind, ensuring species continuity.

**Must know**
• Asexual = one parent, no gamete fusion, identical offspring.
• Fission in Amoeba = binary; yeast/Hydra = budding.
• Asexual is fast and preserves well-adapted genotypes but no variation.
**Reproduction**
• biological process by which living organisms produce **offspring** of their own kind, ensuring **species continuity**.

**Asexual reproduction**
• new individuals from **one parent** only; **no fusion of gametes**; offspring are **genetically identical** (clones); rapid, no need to find a mate.

**Binary fission** (unicellular):
• Parent cell divides into **two equal daughter cells** (e.g. **Amoeba**, bacteria).
• Nucleus divides first (mitosis), then cytoplasm splits.

**Multiple fission**
• nucleus divides repeatedly, then many daughter cells released (e.g. **Plasmodium**).

**Budding**
• small **outgrowth (bud)** on parent body develops into new individual, may detach or remain colonial (e.g. **Hydra**, **yeast**).
• Bud receives nucleus, grows, eventually separates when mature.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Self-pollination vs Cross-pollination**
| Feature | Self-pollination | Cross-pollination |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Pollen transfer | Same flower / same plant | Different plant of same species |
| Variation | Less genetic variation | More genetic variation |
| Agents | Not needed (anthers touch stigma) | Wind, water, insects, birds |
| Examples | Pea, wheat (often) | Maize, papaya, mustard |


**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Asexual Reproduction — Fission & Budding"?
• Explain: Asexual?
• Explain: Fission in Amoeba?
• State: Asexual is fast and preserves well-adapted genotypes but no variation.?
• True or false trap: 'Budding involves meiosis' is FALSE — mitotic divisions only.?
• Exam focus: Name the organism with the method: Amoeba–fission, Hydra–budding, yeast–budding — match-co?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Clone — genetically identical copy of parent.
• Bud — undeveloped outgrowth that can form a new organism.
• Asexual vs sexual: one parent vs two; no variation vs variation; faster vs slower.
• 'Budding involves meiosis' is FALSE — mitotic divisions only.
• Yeast budding leaves a **bud scar** on parent cell.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch2-02 — next topic
teacherTip: Budding = Baby outgrowth; Fission = split into two — picture Hydra with bump, Amoeba pinching in half.
examTip: Name the organism with the method: Amoeba–fission, Hydra–budding, yeast–budding — match-column staple.

content:
**Executive summary:** Fragmentation: parent body breaks into pieces; each fragment grows into a complete new organism (e.g. Spirogyra alga filaments, Planaria flatworm).

**Must know**
• Fragmentation needs no special reproductive organ — just breakage + regeneration.
• Spores = asexual in fungi/ferns; produced in millions for dispersal.
• Vegetative propagation = artificial or natural cloning of crop plants.
**Fragmentation**
• parent body breaks into pieces; each fragment grows into a complete new organism (e.g. **Spirogyra** alga filaments, **Planaria** flatworm).

**Spore formation**
• specialised **reproductive cells (spores)** produced in large numbers inside **sporangia**; light, walled, dispersed by wind/water; germinate in suitable conditions (e.g. **Rhizopus** bread mould, **ferns**, mosses).
• Spores are **not seeds** — unicellular, no stored food reserve like endosperm.

**Vegetative propagation**
• new plants from **vegetative parts** (root, stem, leaf) without seeds or flowers — still asexual.
• **Stem**: runners (strawberry), stolons, tubers (potato — eyes are buds), bulbs (onion), rhizomes (ginger).
• **Root**: sweet potato (adventitious buds on roots), dahlias.
• **Leaf**: **Bryophyllum** — buds (plantlets) on leaf margins.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Fragmentation, Spores & Vegetative Propagation"?
• Explain: Fragmentation needs no special reproductive organ?
• Explain: Spores?
• Explain: Vegetative propagation?
• True or false trap: Spore (single cell, no embryo) ≠ seed (embryo + food + coat).?
• True or false trap: Potato tuber is modified stem, not root — 'eyes' are axillary buds.?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Sporangium — structure producing spores.
• Vegetative propagation — asexual reproduction using non-reproductive plant parts.
• Spore (single cell, no embryo) ≠ seed (embryo + food + coat).
• Potato tuber is modified stem, not root — 'eyes' are axillary buds.
• Bryophyllum plantlets are vegetative, not from flowers.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch2-01 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch2-03 — next topic
teacherTip: Bryophyllum = Babies on leaf margin; Potato eyes = buds on stem tuber — don't call tuber a root.
examTip: Give two examples each of natural vegetative propagation from stem and leaf — strawberry runner + potato tuber; Bryophyllum + Begonia leaf.

content:
**Executive summary:** Artificial vegetative propagation: horticultural methods to grow desirable plants quickly with known traits.

**Must know**
• Grafting combines two plants; cutting/layering uses one plant.
• Scion = upper/desired; Stock = lower/root part.
• Tissue culture needs sterile lab conditions and plant hormones (auxin, cytokinin).
**Artificial vegetative propagation**
• horticultural methods to grow desirable plants quickly with known traits.

**Grafting:**
• Join **scion** (shoot of desired variety) to **stock/rootstock** (root system of hardy variety).
• Vascular cambium of both must align and fuse; tied + sealed until union forms.
• Examples: mango, rose, citrus, apple.
• Stock provides roots/disease resistance; scion provides fruit/flower quality.

**Layering**
• stem still attached to parent while roots form at bent buried portion (e.g. jasmine, grape, bougainvillea).

**Cutting**
• stem/twig planted in moist soil develops adventitious roots (e.g. rose, sugarcane, money plant).

**Tissue culture (micropropagation):**
• Small **explant** (meristem/cell) grown on sterile nutrient medium → **callus** → many plantlets (clones) in weeks.
• Disease-free, rapid mass production (orchids, banana, potato).
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Grafting, Layering & Tissue Culture"?
• State: Grafting combines two plants; cutting/layering uses one plant.?
• Explain: Scion?
• State: Tissue culture needs sterile lab conditions and plant hormones (auxin, cyto?
• True or false trap: 'Grafting works between any two species' is FALSE — close relatives needed for vascul?
• Exam focus: Label scion and stock in graft diagram; state why cambium contact is essential — one mark ?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Scion — grafted shoot bearing desired traits.
• Stock — rooted base providing support and nutrients.
• Explant — small plant tissue used to initiate culture.
• Grafted plant is genetically chimeric — fruit from scion tissue, roots from stock.
• 'Grafting works between any two species' is FALSE — close relatives needed for vascular union.
• Tissue culture is asexual — all plantlets identical.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• TISSUE CULTURE: a technique in which cells or tissues taken under sterile conditions are grown on a culture medium, forming a callus that develops into plantlets and finally new plants.
• Grafting is most suitable as it combines the qualities of two different plants of related species, ensuring better yield and uniform fruit quality.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch2-02 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch2-04 — next topic
teacherTip: Scion on top = Superior fruit; Stock below = Strong roots — S-S mnemonic.
examTip: Label scion and stock in graft diagram; state why cambium contact is essential — one mark each.

content:
**Executive summary:** A flower is the reproductive organ of angiosperms (flowering plants).

**Must know**
• Stamen = male; carpel/pistil = female.
• Ovule inside ovary → after fertilisation becomes seed; ovary wall → fruit.
• Pollen lands on stigma, not ovary directly.
**Calyx (outermost)**
• sepals; usually green; protect flower bud.

**Corolla**
• petals; often coloured/scented; attract pollinators.

**Androecium (male whorl)**
• stamens; each stamen = **filament** + **anther**; anther produces **pollen grains** (male gametophytes / contain male gametes).

**Gynoecium / pistil (female whorl)**
• usually one or more carpels; each carpel has **stigma** (sticky landing platform) + **style** (stalk) + **ovary** (base, contains **ovules** with egg cell / female gamete).

**Complete flower**
• all four whorls present (e.g. mustard, china rose).

**Incomplete flower**
• one or more whorls missing (e.g. corn — no petals).

**Bisexual (hermaphrodite)**
• both androecium and gynoecium (e.g. hibiscus).

**Unisexual**
• staminate (male) or pistillate (female) only (e.g. papaya, watermelon).
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Flower Structure — Four Whorls"?
• Explain: Stamen?
• State: Ovule inside ovary → after fertilisation becomes seed; ovary wall → fruit.?
• State: Pollen lands on stigma, not ovary directly.?
• True or false trap: 'Ovary becomes seed' is FALSE — ovule → seed, ovary → fruit.?
• Exam focus: Draw longitudinal section of bisexual flower labelling all parts — ICSE diagram question a?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Pollen grain — male reproductive unit produced in anther.
• Ovule — structure in ovary containing female gamete (egg).
• Petal attracts; sepal protects — don't swap functions.
• 'Ovary becomes seed' is FALSE — ovule → seed, ovary → fruit.
• Essential whorls = androecium + gynoecium (for reproduction); calyx + corolla = non-essential (accessory).

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• The four whorls of a flower (from outside to inside): 1.
• STAMEN (male): anther + filament.
• (a) attaches the flower to the shoot — PEDICEL (b) the flattened part of the pedicel — THALAMUS (receptacle) (c) the outermost whorl — CALYX (d) the male reprod
• Stamens (male reproductive parts) and carpels (female reproductive parts) are the reproductive whorls.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch2-03 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch2-05 — next topic
teacherTip: From outside in: Calyx, Corolla, Androecium, Gynoecium — CCAG or 'Can Cats Attack Gerbils' for whorl order.
examTip: Draw longitudinal section of bisexual flower labelling all parts — ICSE diagram question almost every cycle.

content:
**Executive summary:** Pollination: transfer of pollen grains from anther to stigma of a flower. Essential BEFORE fertilisation can occur.

**Must know**
• Pollination ≠ fertilisation — pollination is pollen transfer; fertilisation is fusion of gametes inside ovule.
• Insect-pollinated: large colourful flowers; wind-pollinated: inconspicuous, pollen wastage high.
• Cross-pollination increases adaptability of species.
**Pollination**
• transfer of **pollen grains** from **anther** to **stigma** of a flower. Essential BEFORE fertilisation can occur.

**Self-pollination**
• pollen from anther lands on stigma of the **same flower** or **another flower on same plant** (e.g. pea, wheat, rice).
• Economical (little pollen wasted); no pollinator needed; less genetic variation.

**Cross-pollination**
• pollen transferred to stigma of a flower on a **different plant of same species** (e.g. mustard, sunflower, apple).
• More genetic variation; needs agents; pollen produced in large quantities.

**Pollinating agents:**
• **Insects** (entomophily) — bright petals, scent, nectar; sticky/rough pollen; sticky stigma (e.g. rose, jasmine).
• **Wind** (anemophily) — small dull flowers, no nectar; pollen light, smooth, abundant; long hanging anthers; feathery stigma (e.g. grass, maize, pine).
• **Water** (hydrophily) — rare (e.g. Vallisneria).
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Pollination — Self, Cross & Agents"?
• Explain: Pollination ≠ fertilisation?
• State: Insect-pollinated: large colourful flowers; wind-pollinated: inconspicuous,?
• State: Cross-pollination increases adaptability of species.?
• True or false trap: 'Pollen reaches ovule directly' is FALSE — must germinate on stigma, grow pollen tube?
• Exam focus: Tabular compare self vs cross pollination — examiners love 4 differences (agents, pollen n?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Entomophily — insect pollination.
• Anemophily — wind pollination.
• Self-pollination: small pollen number, short filaments; Cross/wind: huge pollen, long exposed anthers.
• 'Pollen reaches ovule directly' is FALSE — must germinate on stigma, grow pollen tube through style.
• Maize tassels (male) and silks (female stigma) — classic wind pollination example.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• SELF-POLLINATION: Transfer of pollen grains from the anthers to the stigma of the same flower, or to another flower of the same plant.
• Wind-pollinated flowers have well-exposed stamens, large feathery stigmas and light, non-sticky pollen; they lack scent and nectar.
• P and Q are NOT suitable for insect pollination.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch2-04 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch2-06 — next topic
teacherTip: Wind = Wasteful pollen, feathery stigma; Insect = Inviting colours, nectar, sticky pollen.
examTip: Tabular compare self vs cross pollination — examiners love 4 differences (agents, pollen number, variation, flower type).

content:
**Executive summary:** Fruit: ripened ovary; may be fleshy (mango, tomato) or dry (mustard, pea pod).

**Must know**
• Fertilisation occurs inside ovule, not on stigma.
• Zygote → embryo; ovule → seed; ovary → fruit.
• Seed dispersal follows fruit/seed maturation.
**Fertilisation (double fertilisation in angiosperms — foundation level):**
• Pollen grain germinates on stigma → **pollen tube** grows down style into ovary.
• Male gamete fuses with **egg cell** in ovule → **zygote** (diploid) → develops into **embryo**.
• (In advanced texts: second male gamete fuses with polar nuclei → endosperm — triploid food tissue.)

**Post-fertilisation changes:**
• **Ovule** → **seed** (contains embryo + stored food + seed coat/testa).
• **Ovary wall** → **fruit** (pericarp); protects seeds, aids dispersal.
• Other floral parts (petals, stamens, style, stigma) usually **wither and fall**.
• **Ovary** alone ripens into fruit; other whorls do not become fruit.

**Fruit**
• ripened ovary; may be fleshy (mango, tomato) or dry (mustard, pea pod).

**True fruit**
• develops only from ovary (apple flesh is thalamus — false fruit accessory).
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Fertilisation & Post-Fertilisation Changes"?
• State: Fertilisation occurs inside ovule, not on stigma.?
• State: Zygote → embryo; ovule → seed; ovary → fruit.?
• State: Seed dispersal follows fruit/seed maturation.?
• True or false trap: 'Petal becomes fruit' is FALSE.?
• Exam focus: Label pollen tube path: stigma → style → ovary → micropyle → synergids/egg — diagram-based?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Zygote — diploid cell formed by fusion of male and female gametes.
• Embryo — young plant in seed with radicle, plumule, cotyledons.
• Pollination (external transfer) happens before fertilisation (internal fusion).
• 'Petal becomes fruit' is FALSE.
• Tomato is a fruit botanically (develops from ovary) though used as vegetable in cooking.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• The four whorls of a flower (from outside to inside): 1.
• After pollination, a pollen tube grows from the stigma, through the style, into the ovary.
• STAMEN (male): anther + filament.
• In an apple the edible fleshy part develops from the thalamus (not the ovary), so it is a false fruit.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch2-05 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch2-07 — next topic
teacherTip: O-S-F chain: Ovule → Seed, Ovary → Fruit — write this chain first in every post-fertilisation answer.
examTip: Label pollen tube path: stigma → style → ovary → micropyle → synergids/egg — diagram-based MCQs common in Olympiads.

content:
**Executive summary:** A seed is a mature ovule containing an embryo ready for dispersal and germination.

**Must know**
• Seed = ovule after fertilisation; dispersal avoids competition with parent.
• Micropyle allows water uptake for imbibition.
• Cotyledons provide food until seedling can photosynthesise.
**Parts of a typical dicot seed (e.g. bean):**
• **Seed coat (testa)** — protective outer layer; **micropyle** — small pore for water entry and oxygen.
• **Cotyledons** — seed leaves; store food (thick in bean) or absorb endosperm (wheat has one cotyledon).
• **Embryo** — **radicle** (future root), **plumule** (future shoot), **hypocotyl**.

**Monocot seed (e.g. maize/corn):**
• Single cotyledon (scutellum); food stored in **endosperm**; embryo small.

**Germination conditions:** water (activates enzymes), oxygen (respiration), suitable temperature; light needed only for some seeds.

**Types:**
• **Epigeal** — cotyledons pushed above ground (bean).
• **Hypogeal** — cotyledons remain underground (pea, maize).
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Seed Structure & Germination"?
• Explain: Seed?
• State: Micropyle allows water uptake for imbibition.?
• State: Cotyledons provide food until seedling can photosynthesise.?
• Exam focus: Identify parts in soaked bean seed dissection — radicle, plumule, cotyledon, testa, microp?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Germination — resumption of growth of embryo into seedling.
• Imbibition — absorption of water causing seed to swell.
• Dicot = two cotyledons; Monocot = one cotyledon.
• 'Seed and fruit develop simultaneously' — fruit often encloses seed.
• Dormancy — seed waits for favourable season before germinating.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• WHY: seeds are dispersed to far-off places to reduce competition for sunlight, water, minerals and space, so the same plants can grow in different places.
• MCQ key: **Only P and R** — P and R support it.
• After pollination, a pollen tube grows from the stigma, through the style, into the ovary.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch2-06 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch2-08 — next topic
teacherTip: Radicle Roots first, Plumule Pushes shoot — R-before-P in germination order.
examTip: Identify parts in soaked bean seed dissection — radicle, plumule, cotyledon, testa, micropyle labels expected.

content:
**Executive summary:** Seed dispersal: scattering of seeds away from parent plant to reduce competition for light, water, minerals and space; colonise new habitats.

**Must know**
• Each adaptation matches dispersal agent (wing → wind, hook → animal fur).
• Dispersal increases survival and species spread.
• Fruit aids animal dispersal by being colourful, tasty, nutritious.
**Seed dispersal**
• scattering of seeds away from parent plant to reduce competition for light, water, minerals and space; colonise new habitats.

**By wind (anemochory):**
• Light seeds, winged (maple, drumstick/Moringa), hairy parachutes (dandelion, cotton, calotropis).

**By water (hydrochory):**
• Fibrous husk, air spaces — coconut floats on sea currents for long distances.

**By animals (zoochory):**
• Hooks/spines stick to fur (Xanthium, burdock); edible fruits — seeds pass through gut unharmed (tomato, guava) or dropped after eating.

**By explosive mechanism (autochory):**
• Sudden drying causes pod to split/shatter flinging seeds (balsam 'touch-me-not', pea, castor).

**By humans/agencies:**
• Agriculture, trade, accidental transport.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Seed Dispersal — Agents & Adaptations"?
• State: Each adaptation matches dispersal agent (wing → wind, hook → animal fur).?
• State: Dispersal increases survival and species spread.?
• State: Fruit aids animal dispersal by being colourful, tasty, nutritious.?
• True or false trap: Coconut = water dispersal, not wind (heavy with fibrous coat for floating).?
• Exam focus: Match seed/fruit to agent: maple–wind, coconut–water, Xanthium–animal, balsam–explosion — ?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Dispersal — transport of seeds/ fruits away from parent plant.
• Autochory — self-dispersal by explosive fruits.
• Coconut = water dispersal, not wind (heavy with fibrous coat for floating).
• Cotton fibres aid wind dispersal of seeds.
• 'All fruits are dispersed by animals' is FALSE.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• WHY: seeds are dispersed to far-off places to reduce competition for sunlight, water, minerals and space, so the same plants can grow in different places.
• Dandelion seeds have light, feathery parachute-like structures that help them float in the wind over long distances.
• MCQ key: **Only P and R** — P and R support it.
• Grafting is most suitable as it combines the qualities of two different plants of related species, ensuring better yield and uniform fruit quality.
• After pollination, a pollen tube grows from the stigma, through the style, into the ovary.
• Incorrect statement: Understand how grass plants which are planted at some distance from each other.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch2-07 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch2-09 — next topic
examTip: Match seed/fruit to agent: maple–wind, coconut–water, Xanthium–animal, balsam–explosion — pure recall MCQs.

subtopic: 9. Sexual vs Asexual Reproduction in Plants
content:
**Executive summary:** | Feature | Asexual | Sexual | | Mode | Vegetative parts, spores, fission, budding | Flowers, gametes, seeds | | Parents | One | Two (male + female gametes) | | Variation | None (clones) | Present | | Speed | Fast | Slower (needs pollination + seed) | | Examples | Potato, rose cutting, fern spores | Mustard, mango (from seed), pea |

**Must know**
• Higher plants reproduce both ways; farmers use asexual for clones, sexual for new varieties.
• Variation from sexual reproduction is raw material for natural selection.
• Hybrid seeds (sexual) may not breed true in next generation.
**Sexual reproduction in plants**
• involves **formation of gametes**, **pollination**, **fertilisation**; offspring show **genetic variation**; slower but enables evolution and adaptation.

**Asexual reproduction**
• no gametes, no fertilisation; **one parent**; **identical** offspring; rapid, used for stable crop varieties.

**When each is advantageous:**
• Asexual — preserve hybrid quality, rapid orchard expansion, no pollinator needed.
• Sexual — new combinations of genes, seed dormancy survives harsh seasons, long-distance dispersal.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Sexual vs Asexual reproduction in plants**
| Feature | Sexual | Asexual |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Gametes | Two fuse (fertilisation) | Not involved |
| Genetic variation | Yes — new combinations | No — clone of parent |
| Speed | Slower (flower → seed) | Faster (runner, bud, graft) |
| Examples | Maize, pea, mango | Potato, Bryophyllum, yeast |


**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Sexual vs Asexual Reproduction in Plants"?
• State: Higher plants reproduce both ways; farmers use asexual for clones, sexual f?
• State: Variation from sexual reproduction is raw material for natural selection.?
• State: Hybrid seeds (sexual) may not breed true in next generation.?
• Exam focus: Give two advantages of each type — standard 4-mark question; mention variation for sexual,?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Gamete — haploid reproductive cell (pollen/egg in plants).
• Hybrid — offspring from parents of different varieties/species with mixed traits.
• 'Asexual reproduction involves mitosis only' — TRUE for cell division aspect.
• 'Sexual reproduction always needs flowers' — TRUE for angiosperms; ferns use spores + fertilisation (alternation of generations).
• Seed formation requires fertilisation; tuber sprouting does not.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch2-08 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch2-10 — next topic
teacherTip: Sexual = Shuffle genes (variation); Asexual = Xerox copy (identical).
examTip: Give two advantages of each type — standard 4-mark question; mention variation for sexual, speed and clone fidelity for asexual.

subtopic: 10. Complete Reproductive Cycle — Flower to New Plant
content:
**Executive summary:** 1.  Flower develops on mature plant.

**Must know**
• Correct sequence: pollination BEFORE fertilisation; fertilisation BEFORE seed/fruit formation.
• Fruit protects and helps disperse seeds.
• Full cycle links reproduction to species survival and spread.
**Life cycle summary (angiosperm):**

**Alternation in farmers' practice:**
• Mango grafting (asexual) for known sweet variety.
• Mustard/wheat from seed (sexual) each season.
• Banana — sterile seeds, propagated vegetatively (suckers).
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Complete Reproductive Cycle — Flower to New Plant"?
• State: Correct sequence: pollination BEFORE fertilisation; fertilisation BEFORE se?
• State: Fruit protects and helps disperse seeds.?
• State: Full cycle links reproduction to species survival and spread.?
• True or false trap: Order trap: fertilisation cannot occur before pollination.?
• Exam focus: Arrange jumbled stages questions — number pollination as step before fertilisation; seed f?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• Life cycle — series of stages from one generation to the next.
• Seedling — young plant after germination.
• Order trap: fertilisation cannot occur before pollination.
• 'Fruit formation without fertilisation' — parthenocarpic fruits (banana, some oranges) possible but no true seeds.
• Dispersal is NOT the same as germination.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• After pollination, a pollen tube grows from the stigma, through the style, into the ovary.
• Incorrect statement: Asexual reproduction, on the other hand, is the type of reproduction that does not involve the fusion of gametes.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch2-09 — previous topic
teacherTip: Write the 7-step chain on flashcard: Flower → Pollen/Ovule → Pollination → Pollen tube → Fertilisation → Seed/Fruit → Dispersal → Germination.
examTip: Arrange jumbled stages questions — number pollination as step before fertilisation; seed formation after fertilisation only.
